data.title: 2 Attractive Infrastructure-Focused CEFs For Monthly Payouts
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Infrastructure is critical to everyday life, but it isn't always something that most people in the developed world think about until it isn't functioning as intended. Whether that be construction on roadways, bridges, airports or power outages caused by downed powerlines or pipeline bursts, it is typically an afterthought until something is wrong. I might be the only one, but after a big snowstorm when the power goes out, there have been a number of times I've walked into a room and tried to turn on a light switch while fully being aware that I'm already using a flashlight. It just becomes such a habit.

Given the critical nature and importance of relying on these infrastructure projects, they tend to come with very steady cash flows. Particularly utility companies, but also pipelines that act like tollways for fossil fuels such as oil and natural gas, and whatever other commodity can be shoved through them...or not so much commodities such as waste like sewage.

With that said, we are highlighting two closed-end funds today that provide exposure to this area of our world that are trading at attractive discounts.

#1 Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation (TYG)

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.98
  • Discount/Premium: -9.95%
  • Distribution Yield: 10.02%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.69%
  • Leverage: 20%
  • Managed Assets: $1.1 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

TYG mentions that the fund is designed to have "exposure to energy infrastructure, long-lived and essential, midstream power and renewable assets." These companies include those that "generate, transport and distribute electricity as well as process, store, distribute and market natural gas, natural gas liquids, refined products, and crude oil." It also has an investment objective to seek a "high level of total return, emphasizing current distributions."

In the closed-end fund structure, there is no K-1 or unrelated business taxable income (UBTI). That means they can be more appropriate for "IRA and tax-exempt suitability."

TYG has been on a bit of a transformation since our last update. That was in July 2024, and we noted that a distribution boost could be ahead based on their 7-10% NAV distribution rate policy. That ultimately did occur when they announced a 10% increase in the payout in August 2024.

However, they then announced a massive 40% increase in December and switched to a monthly distribution schedule. At this time, the NAV distribution rate sits at around 9%, so still within the fund's 7-10% target outlined previously.

This big boost wasn't out of nowhere, though; it was on the back of the merger taking place between TYG and its sister fund, Tortoise Midstream Energy Fund (NTG). That helped to increase the size of TYG quite substantially and pushed it over the $1 billion in total managed assets level, which includes its leverage as well.

Some of this move was likely the result of pressure from Saba Capital Management. Saba is a well-known activist group in the CEF space. They hold a nearly 8% stake in the fund, though they've been selling, as it was up to a 10% stake previously.

TYG, being the surviving fund, means NTG is now defunct, and all history is going to be what TYG had. That history includes, the devastating drop the fund experienced during Covid. This fund was more heavily leveraged and, more specifically, focused on energy infrastructure at that time. The fund still carries a heavy leaning toward energy infrastructure.

That said, they've also opened up the fund to be more diversified—that also includes some renewable energy exposure, as we can see listed above. In the energy space, the larger companies almost all have some renewable operations within their more diversified lines of business as well.

The fund also holds some utility exposure as well, for even more diversified infrastructure exposure. They really aren't so limited these days to being specifically focused on energy investments, as they were prior to Covid.

In looking at the latest top ten, we can see quite a concentration in just a handful of names. Overall, the top ten account for almost 64% of the fund's total invested assets.

Leverage has been more modest since the big crash, but any leverage is going to increase risks as the upside might be enhanced, but downside moves are also amplified.

In terms of the fund's discount, it has been narrowing quite materially over the last year. That meaningful distribution increase and a change to the monthly distribution policy likely helped to result in this occurring.

Still, TYG is right around our 'Buy' target of a 10% discount, so it is close enough to still be a solid choice.

#2 abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (ASGI)

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.23
  • Discount/Premium: -8.67%
  • Distribution Yield: 12.36%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.83%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $587.2 million
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation date around July 28, 2035).

ASGI's investment objective is “to seek to provide a high level of total return with an emphasis on current income.”

To achieve this objective, the investment strategy is basic. They will “invest in a portfolio of income-producing public and private infrastructure equity investments from around the world.”

On more of a side note, abrdn has moved back to Aberdeen in terms of their brand name. They had dropped the vowels in 2021. I suspect that ASGI will get its vowels back as well. This isn't fundamental to the fund, but worth noting, as it could cause a bit of confusion when names keep switching.

An additional change that is in the pipeline for ASGI is that it is set to potentially absorb abrdn Japan Equity Fund (JEQ). This would initially seem like an unusual fit. Still, like the other funds that ASGI has absorbed over the years, these positions will be liquidated and invested under ASGI's investment policy. Ultimately, this means that it is a neutral to slightly positive move for ASGI. The slight positive is that it can become a larger fund, adding to potentially more liquidity as daily trading volume can increase.

That said, JEQ has less than $100 million in net assets, which excludes their borrowings. If JEQ shareholders approve of the merger, the fund will also be conducting a tender offer for 50% of outstanding shares at 98% of NAV. So, roughly speaking, ASGI will see about a $50 million boost in net asset value potentially. Not nothing, but not all that significant either to meaningfully move the needle.

With that out of the way, this remains an interesting infrastructure-focused fund as it takes a more global approach, adding to the manager's flexibility on where to invest. At this time, they listed that about 56% of their portfolio is listed as "North America," which is generally going to be dominated by U.S. exposure.

Even further, the fund holds some private investments that can help to differentiate the fund from its infrastructure peers as well. As of their last annual report, they listed that private placements represented around 12.3% of the portfolio. These are spread between both credit and equity private investments, but mostly equity. These are also listed as level 3 securities, which means that valuation can be hard to nail down exactly.

Ultimately, it takes some assumptions and running what comparisons they can do, but it is only a best guess. The real value would only be known once an investment IPOs, or they go to sell the position and find out what one is willing to pay for the asset.

Finally, another differentiating factor for ASGI is the fund's largest sector exposure comes from the "industrials" sector. That's probably not what first comes to mind when you think of infrastructure—which is more commonly associated with utilities, energy, real estate and even communication services in some cases. That's where something like Aena S.M.E., S.A. (OTCPK:ANYYY) is listed as being in the industrial sector. More specifically, the airport services industry, and they are headquartered in Spain.

In terms of private investment exposure, that can contribute to some of the fund's discount, which continues to trade wider after a rather brief period of seeing it narrowing.

Helping to drive that discount narrowing was the fund implementing a series of distribution increases based on a distribution policy change. The latest policy is set at 12% of NAV, reset monthly. This is quite an elevated level when you also have to factor in the operating expenses of the fund on top of this.

That ends up meaning the fund has to generate between 13 and 14% returns from their underlying portfolio. That isn't impossible, but over the longer term it becomes more and more improbable. So far, the fund has been able to mostly maintain it right around the $0.20 per month distribution level.

Of course, they are always able to change their distribution policy. Further, if NAV is falling, that means the distribution paid out is also falling and that can help to slow down potential NAV erosion. Despite my being rather skeptical that they can maintain the NAV with a 12% distribution NAV policy, total returns can still be quite attractive. I believe that the discount and underlying investment exposure make it a worthwhile choice in the infrastructure CEF space.

data.title: S&P 500 On Track To $4,800s Before EOY: Here Is Why I'm Shorting U.S. Equities
data.description:
Liberation Day is finally here, and the tariff confusion has mostly cleared up.

Or has it?

At the time of writing this article, the futures market took a hit across the board, with the Dow Jones (INDU) down 2.7%, the S&P 500 (SPX) off by 3.4%, and the Nasdaq-100 (US100:IND) falling 3.8%. In the bonds market, the 2-year yield (US2Y) is now down to 3.80% from 3.95% before the tariff announcement.

Now that the uncertainty is out of the way, I believe there will be a non-zero amount of contrarian investors who will jump in and happily buy the dip today. To me, it's going to be tough to watch that happen. Why?

We do have a better handle on tariffs now. That doesn't mean the figures on the table below are encouraging. Far from that.

Keep in mind that these reciprocal tariffs (effective April 9) come on top of earlier ones. That means imports from China now face a total tariff of 54% (20% + 34%), and European car imports (among many other examples) are hit with a 45% tariff (25% + 20%).

According to data from the UN Comtrade database on international trade, here are the top countries based on the share of total goods imported into the U.S. (table below by Trading Economics).

Considering the reciprocal tariff agenda and the top countries importing goods into the U.S., excluding Canada and Mexico (more on this in just a moment), the lowest tariff among these top 10 importers is 20% on German and Italian imports, and the highest is 46% on Vietnam imports.

That said, Mexico and Canada were exempted from Trump's new 10% global tariff baseline, although previous tariffs of 25% on non-USMCA compliant goods and 10% on non-USMCA compliant energy and potash will remain in place. According to the White House fact sheet,

"In the event the existing fentanyl/migration IEEPA orders are terminated, USMCA compliant goods would continue to receive preferential treatment, while non-USMCA compliant goods would be subject to a 12% reciprocal tariff."

A final note on Mexican and Canadian imports. Some sources estimate that roughly 50% of all goods imported from Mexico are compliant with the USMCA agreement. For Canada, this value is closer to 38% of all goods imported into the US. That said, I wouldn't be surprised if Canadian and Mexican companies put in the effort to check whether their products qualify under USMCA. I believe many haven't done this due to the time and investment required. If they do, I think we'll see fewer imports from these two countries getting hit with the 25% tariff.

The takeaway here is pretty simple: expect the inflation rate to rise in the near term.

When exactly? I believe March data should reflect a small tick in inflation due to the 25% tariffs on non-USMCA compliant imports, the 25% on steel and aluminum, and the 20% on Chinese imports. That said, I expect this uptick to be noise when compared to the April and May CPI data.

Therefore, I'd keep a close watch on the following dates to see the actual impact of these tariffs on inflation:

  • March CPI data: Apr. 10, 2025
  • April CPI data: May 13, 2025
  • May CPI data: Jun. 11, 2025.

What's the issue with seeing an uptick in inflation anyways? Well, it seems that in the last FOMC meeting in March, Jerome Powell signaled two potential rate cuts of 25 basis points each later this year. In my view, that's a mistake. Waiting for inflation to tick up before cutting rates is more like slamming the brakes after you've already missed the exit. Some analysts have recently projected three rate cuts for this year, while the CME FedWatch consensus projects four rate cuts as the most likely scenario by January 2026.

Let me be clear. The reason why I'm unhappy with the reactive approach of the Fed is due to the impact that DOGE initiatives will have on employment. To be even more clear, I foresee unemployment ticking up way before the Fed decides to increase the number of rate cuts. Why? Because, as I said before, inflation may rise with the new tariffs. If you lower interest rates to save jobs, you are adding fuel to the inflation problem driven by tariffs. And, bear in mind, this inflation is not driven by an overly stimulated economy; it's driven by a (likely) global trade war.

Coming back to DOGE, Musk is planning to cut $2 trillion in federal spending, most likely by mid-2026. What concerns me is that, so far, only 7% of the total $2T goal has been achieved. Yet, the HHS plans to cut 10,000 jobs. In March, the Education Department said it was going to cut half its staff, SBA is cutting 43%, and VA will lay off 80,000 workers.

I have a high conviction that the private sector will not be able to fully absorb these workers. Why? Because I believe the private sector will absorb some of the additional costs the new tariffs will bring. In fact, I expect private sector hiring to stall until the Fed brings interest rates below 4%.

Some might say the latest jobless claims report is not alarming, but keep in mind that DOGE has barely started its cost-cutting plans, with only 7% of the $2 trillion target reached so far.

As you may know, periods of high inflation and high unemployment are typically known as stagflation. In fact, I missed an element in the last sentence: stagnant economic growth.

That leads me to my bear case.

My bear case has four simple pillars:

  1. I believe the inflation rate in the US will rise due to the new tariff agenda. This will negatively impact both consumer spending and earnings, as I believe some corporations will bite the bullet and not pass 100% of the tariff increments to their customers.
  2. I believe DOGE cutting initiatives will pressure employment. So far, we have seen only the tip of the iceberg, with 7% of the $2T target achieved. I believe the unemployment in the government sector will overshadow the new employment generated in the private sector as US equities will have to deal with either less demand as prices increase or fewer profits as they absorb some of these tariffs.
  3. I expect GDP growth to drop sharply in Q1 this year, with a chance of negative numbers in Q2 and Q3. I believe the main reason for this possible decline is that consumer spending is likely to slow down due to the new tariffs.
  4. I think the Fed will probably sit on the sidelines and watch how things play out. I expect a late reaction in interest cuts, possibly after unemployment and inflation rates tick up. Lowering the interest rates too much will negatively affect inflation (which is artificially inflated by tariffs). Therefore, a period of stagflation in 2025 is more than likely.

Here is a quick note on GDP before I reveal some of the positions in my two investment portfolios. I don't expect Q1's GDP change to be in the negative values, as noted by Atlanta's GDPNow -3.7% estimate.

If we look at the math behind this estimate, one can clearly notice that CIPI (change in private inventories) has not been updated since the front-loading effect was seen at the end of February.

As seen in the last column of the table above, the CIPI has not yet integrated the increase in imports. By the time new economic data is released, I expect this estimate to be in the positive values. A quick look at the New York Fed Nowcast shows a 68% chance for Q1's GDP to come in between 1.34% and 4.37%.

Here are some important dates to keep in mind in regard to GDP:

  • April 30: Gross Domestic Product, Q1 2025 (BEA's Advance Estimate)
  • July 30: Gross Domestic Product, Q2 2025 (BEA's Advance Estimate)
  • October 30: Gross Domestic Product, Q3 2025 (BEA's Advance Estimate).

Usually, the advance estimate doesn't differ much from the remaining two revisions, so those are the dates that I would keep in mind.

Outlook

By the time I expect the Fed to react and ramp up their interest rate cut plans, I believe the S&P 500 will be trading close to the $4,800s. That is my price target for the rock bottom of this selloff. Why?

Take a look at where the past two selloffs (arrows 1 & 3 below) have found rock bottom.

Exactly! The rock bottom was reached at price levels that were the highs in the previous expansion cycles. The 2022 selloff (1) found rock bottom at the same price level as the peak prior to the 2020 selloff (2). Same with the pandemic selloff (3) finding rock bottom at an old resistance level in 2015 (4).

For this (annoyingly) simple reason, I project a rock bottom for the 2025 selloff to be in the vicinity of $4,800s.

With pessimism picking up momentum, I believe a selloff of this magnitude is inevitable. I will be honest: in the near term (i.e., before the end of 2025), I don't see any positive announcement, other than a slim chance of Trump pulling back on his tariff plans, that could turn around the pessimism in the markets.

In the meantime, I am shorting though puts the following American equities (most of these bets were placed during the first week of March):

RCAT (Jul 18 2025, $5 puts), QUBT (Jul 18 2025, $5 puts), QBTS (Jul 18 2025, $7 puts and Apr 17 2025, $8 puts), IONQ (Jul 18 2025, $20 puts), DNN (Jul 18 2025, $1.5 puts), JOBY (Jul 18 2025, $6 puts), CLSK (Jul 18 2025, $7 puts), BBAI (Jun 20 2025, $4.5 puts).

At the time of writing this article, the only two long positions I hold are shares in NBIS and BTG. Please note that these two companies, along with DNN, are not based in the US.

I may roll out some of the puts near the expiration dates, I may add new bets, I may take profits, or I may leave everything as is, depending on how the markets react to the tariffs in the next few weeks. That said, my conviction for the $4,800s rock bottom is quite high.

An important reminder: past performance not indicative of future results. The downside of my bets (not to be confused with investments) is 100%. I might lose it all, and I'm okay with that. Every speculator has their own level of risk tolerance. Mine might be significantly different from yours.

Risks

Over the past decade, buying the dip in US equities has worked out (most of the time). If I were a patient investor instead of a speculator, I'd buy the dip now, delete my Interactive Brokers app, so I wouldn't keep checking my portfolio this year, and ask my partner to shut off my monitor the moment I even think about opening a financial website. Unfortunately, I'm not like that.

That said, timing the bottom (which is what I am trying to do here) has rarely worked out for analysts trying to guess how much further things will drop before buying the dip actually makes sense.

I admit that out of all the factors that could turn around investors' sentiment in US equities, I believe Trump putting a pause on his tariff policy is the single most important factor to consider. If other countries don't hit back and trade talks go well for the US, I think there's a non-zero chance this could happen.

That said, whether this event happens, or when it might happen, is anyone's guess. My guess is that such an event is unlikely this year. Therefore, I'm sticking with my strong sell rating.
